Bash
{
  "command": "cd /d/xampp/htdocs/nudge\ngit commit -m \"$(cat <<'EOF'\nAdd blocker triage and mailto draft flow\n\nImplements CHKP-FUNC-004 through 009: a check-in screen (Submit\nProof / I'm Blocked), a four-option triage (10-minute focus timer\nplus three blocker reasons that generate editable mail drafts), and\nthe mailto: send-and-pause flow that optimistically sets a task to\nOn Hold independent of whether the email actually gets sent. No\nstored contacts or drafts, per the communication-architecture\ndecision doc — recipients are typed fresh each time.\n\nAlso folds in a round of layout fixes from manual review: full-width\nCheck-In button, Mark Complete restyled to a secondary/outline\nbutton so it stops competing visually with the page's primary\nactions, and the Status card merged into Attachments since it held\nno information of its own, only an action.\nEOF\n)\" 2>&1\ngit push 2>&1",
  "description": "Commit and push Step 4"
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:02

Yes — Step 5 is where Part C (the notification-worker) begins: REVIEW-FUNC-001/002, a standalone process that polls the core-app's REST API. Let's build it.

Claude12.07.2026 15:02
Bash
{
  "command": "ls /d/xampp/htdocs/nudge/notification-worker 2>&1\nmkdir -p /d/xampp/htdocs/nudge/notification-worker",
  "description": "Confirm notification-worker directory is empty, ensure it exists"
}
Tool result
(Bash completed with no output)
Claude12.07.2026 15:02
Write
{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "content": "import json\nimport time\nimport urllib.error\nimport urllib.request\nfrom datetime import datetime\n\nCORE_APP_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:3000\"\nPOLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 30\n\n\ndef log(message: str) -> None:\n    print(f\"[{datetime.now().isoformat(timespec='seconds')}] {message}\")\n\n\ndef fetch_open_tasks() -> list[dict] | None:\n    url = f\"{CORE_APP_URL}/api/tasks\"\n    try:\n        with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=5) as response:\n            tasks = json.loads(response.read())\n    except urllib.error.URLError as error:\n        log(f\"Could not reach core-app at {CORE_APP_URL}: {error}\")\n        return None\n\n    return [task for task in tasks if task[\"status\"] == \"active\"]\n\n\ndef run() -> None:\n    log(f\"notification-worker started, polling {CORE_APP_URL} every {POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS}s\")\n    while True:\n        tasks = fetch_open_tasks()\n        if tasks is not None:\n            names = [task[\"name\"] for task in tasks]\n            log(f\"{len(tasks)} open task(s): {names}\")\n        time.sleep(POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS)\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    run()\n"
}
Tool result
File created successfully at: D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\notification-worker\worker.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
Claude12.07.2026 15:03

{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\requirements.txt",
  "content": "# No external dependencies yet. REVIEW-NFR-001 requires stdlib-only\n# smtplib/imaplib once email sending/reading is added (Step 6+); this\n# step's REST polling also uses stdlib (urllib.request), so nothing\n# to install for now. Kept as a placeholder for consistency with\n# core-app's per-process requirements.txt.\n"
}

ProcessId CommandLine                                                                 

--------- -----------                                                                 

    15204 D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\notification-worker\.venv\Scripts\python.exe worker.py

Claude12.07.2026 15:06

Good catch waiting to happen — the log file is empty even though the process is confirmed running. That's Python's stdout buffering when output isn't a terminal (block-buffered instead of line-buffered), so print() calls sit in memory until the buffer fills or the process exits. Real usage (redirecting to a log file) would hit the same issue. Let me fix it properly in the code.

Claude12.07.2026 15:06
PowerShell
{
  "command": "$procs = Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter \"Name='python.exe'\" | Where-Object { $_.CommandLine -like '*notification-worker*' }\nforeach ($p in $procs) { Stop-Process -Id $p.ProcessId -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }\nStart-Sleep -Seconds 1\n\"stopped\"",
  "description": "Stop the worker processes before fixing buffering"
}
Tool result
stopped
Claude12.07.2026 15:06
Edit
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "old_string": "def log(message: str) -> None:\n    print(f\"[{datetime.now().isoformat(timespec='seconds')}] {message}\")",
  "new_string": "def log(message: str) -> None:\n    print(f\"[{datetime.now().isoformat(timespec='seconds')}] {message}\", flush=True)"
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:09
Write
{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\08-step5-notification-worker-skeleton.md",
  "content": "# Step 5: Notification-Worker-Grundgerüst (Teil C, Start)\n\n**Status:** Abgeschlossen\n**Bezug:** REVIEW-FUNC-001, REVIEW-FUNC-002. Ab hier beginnt Teil C (verteiltes System, siehe `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`).\n\n---\n\n## Was gebaut wurde\n\n```\nnotification-worker/\n├── worker.py           eigenständiger Prozess, pollt die Kern-App-API periodisch\n├── requirements.txt    aktuell leer (keine externen Abhängigkeiten nötig)\n└── .venv/                eigene virtuelle Umgebung, getrennt von core-app/.venv\n```\n\n### `worker.py`\n\n```python\nCORE_APP_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:3000\"\nPOLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 30\n\n\ndef fetch_open_tasks() -> list[dict] | None:\n    url = f\"{CORE_APP_URL}/api/tasks\"\n    try:\n        with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=5) as response:\n            tasks = json.loads(response.read())\n    except urllib.error.URLError as error:\n        log(f\"Could not reach core-app at {CORE_APP_URL}: {error}\")\n        return None\n    return [task for task in tasks if task[\"status\"] == \"active\"]\n\n\ndef run() -> None:\n    log(f\"notification-worker started, polling {CORE_APP_URL} every {POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS}s\")\n    while True:\n        tasks = fetch_open_tasks()\n        if tasks is not None:\n            log(f\"{len(tasks)} open task(s): {[t['name'] for t in tasks]}\")\n        time.sleep(POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS)\n```\n\n- **HTTP-Client:** `urllib.request` aus der Standardbibliothek statt `requests`. REVIEW-NFR-001 verlangt Stdlib-only explizit nur für SMTP/IMAP (kommt in Schritt 6), aber da für eine einzelne periodische GET-Anfrage kein externes Paket nötig ist, bleibt `requirements.txt` komplett leer — keine einzige Abhängigkeit im gesamten Worker-Prozess bislang. Passt zur \"Minimal-Toollandschaft\"-Leitlinie aus `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`.\n- **Cron-Ersatz:** Einfache `while True` / `time.sleep(30)`-Schleife statt einer zusätzlichen `schedule`-Bibliothek — laut Kickoff-Briefing explizit als gleichwertige Option vorgesehen (\"Cron-artiger Trigger, z. B. schedule-Library oder einfache Loop mit time.sleep\"). Eine Zeile Standardbibliothek ist einfacher zu erklären als eine zusätzliche Abhängigkeit.\n- **Fehlerbehandlung:** Ein nicht erreichbarer core-app-Prozess führt zu einer geloggten `URLError`, nicht zu einem Absturz — direkte Umsetzung von REVIEW-NFR-002 (\"Ausfall eines Prozesses darf den anderen nicht zum Absturz bringen\"), hier aus Sicht des Workers.\n- **`flush=True` beim Logging:** Python puffert `stdout` blockweise, sobald es nicht an ein Terminal, sondern (wie hier beim Testen) in eine Datei umgeleitet wird. Ohne `flush=True` wären Log-Zeilen erst beim Prozessende sichtbar geworden — beim ersten Testlauf tatsächlich aufgefallen (leere Logdatei trotz laufendem Prozess) und sofort behoben.\n\n### Eigener Scope-Hinweis: \"offene Tasks\" statt \"Fokus-Tasks\"\n\nREVIEW-FUNC-002 spricht von \"offenen **Fokus**-Tasks\" — der Fokus-Tasks-Begriff (die drei wöchentlich committeten Tasks) entsteht aber erst durch REVIEW-FUNC-006 (Rückspielen der Priorisierung), das laut Bau-Reihenfolge erst in Schritt 8 kommt. Für diesen Schritt pollt der Worker deshalb **alle aktiven Tasks** (`status == \"active\"`) — die Filterung auf tatsächliche Fokus-Tasks kommt automatisch dazu, sobald das Feld dafür existiert (Schritt 8). Kein Nacharbeiten nötig, nur eine Erweiterung der Filterbedingung in `fetch_open_tasks()`.\n\n### Bewusst nicht gebaut\n\nKein eigener `/health`-Endpunkt für den Worker (der zweite Ausbau-Schritt aus `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`, Abschnitt 3) — dafür müsste der Worker selbst einen HTTP-Server starten, was ihn von einem reinen Polling-Skript zu einem zweiten Webservice machen würde. Laut Doku reicht für die Abgabe \"Stufe 1\" (manueller Beweis per Log-Beobachtung), was in diesem Schritt bereits erbracht wurde (siehe Verifikation).\n\n## Verifikation: Nachweis der Prozess-Unabhängigkeit (REVIEW-NFR-002, vorgezogen)\n\nObwohl der formale Nachweis erst Schritt 9 ist, wurde die Kernaussage schon hier direkt getestet, weil sie sich unmittelbar aus dem gebauten Code ergibt:\n\n1. core-app gestartet, ein Test-Task angelegt.\n2. Worker gestartet → pollt sofort, loggt `1 open task(s): ['Worker Poll Test']`. Zweiter Poll-Zyklus nach 30s bestätigt dasselbe Ergebnis.\n3. core-app-Prozess hart beendet (`Stop-Process`).\n4. Nächster Poll-Zyklus des Workers: `Could not reach core-app at http://127.0.0.1:3000: <urlopen error [WinError 10061] ...>` — **kein Absturz**, die Schleife läuft unverändert weiter (im nächsten Zyklus derselbe Fehler, sauber wiederholt).\n5. core-app neu gestartet, ohne den Worker anzufassen.\n6. Nächster Poll-Zyklus des Workers verbindet sich automatisch wieder erfolgreich, ohne Neustart des Worker-Prozesses nötig.\n\nDamit ist die für Teil C zentrale Anforderung — zwei unabhängige Prozesse, Ausfall des einen bringt den anderen nicht zum Absturz — bereits im Kern demonstriert. Schritt 9 liefert dafür noch den formalen Screenshot-/GIF-Nachweis für die Abgabe.\n\nBeide Prozesse danach gestoppt, Testdatenbank gelöscht.\n\n## Nächster Schritt\n\nSchritt 6: E-Mail-Versand + Mailpit-Anbindung (REVIEW-FUNC-003) — der Worker verschickt eine Mail mit allen offenen Fokus-Tasks über `smtplib`, lokal gegen Mailpit getestet.\n"
}

{
  "command": "cd /d/xampp/htdocs/nudge\ngit add notification-worker/ docs/08-step5-notification-worker-skeleton.md\ngit commit -m \"$(cat <<'EOF'\nAdd notification-worker skeleton as an independent process\n\nImplements REVIEW-FUNC-001/002: a standalone Python process, own\nvenv, zero external dependencies (urllib.request for polling,\nstdlib-only in line with REVIEW-NFR-001). Polls core-app's\n/api/tasks every 30s via a plain time.sleep loop and logs open\ntasks.\n\nManually verified REVIEW-NFR-002 ahead of schedule: killed core-app\nmid-run, confirmed the worker logs a connection error and keeps\npolling without crashing, then restarted core-app and confirmed the\nworker reconnects on its own on the next cycle with no intervention.\n\nAlso fixes a buffering bug found during that test: print() output\nwasn't flushed when redirected to a log file, so the worker looked\ndead even though it was running.\nEOF\n)\" 2>&1\ngit push 2>&1",

Claude12.07.2026 15:11

Step 4 and Step 5 are both committed and pushed.

Step 5 summary — yes, this is where Part C (the distributed-system requirement) starts:


  • [notification-worker/worker.py](notification-worker/worker.py) — a fully independent process (own venv, zero dependencies) that polls core-app's /api/tasks every 30s over plain urllib.request

  • [docs/08-step5-notification-worker-skeleton.md](docs/08-step5-notification-worker-skeleton.md)

I actually proved the process-independence claim live rather than just asserting it, since it's cheap to check right after building the code: started both processes, confirmed the worker picks up tasks, killed core-app mid-run, confirmed the worker logs a clean connection error and keeps polling instead of crashing, then restarted core-app and confirmed the worker reconnects on its own with zero intervention. That's the core of REVIEW-NFR-002 (formal write-up with screenshots is still Step 9, but the mechanism is now demonstrated and documented).

One real bug I hit and fixed along the way: the worker's log output wasn't showing up in its log file at all even though the process was running — Python buffers stdout differently when it's redirected to a file vs. a terminal, so print() calls were sitting in memory instead of writing through. Added flush=True, otherwise anyone running this with > worker.log & (the exact way the docs describe running it) would see an empty log and think it was dead.

Also worth knowing: I found 14 leftover zombie Python processes from earlier testing sessions this conversation (old server/worker instances that survived my kill attempts). All cleaned up now, but if your machine ever feels like something's stuck on port 3000, check Task Manager for stray python.exe processes — I'll be more careful about full cleanup going forward.

Ready for Step 6: email sending + Mailpit (REVIEW-FUNC-003) whenever you want to continue — that's where the worker starts actually emailing the weekly focus-task list via smtplib. Since that needs Mailpit running locally as the SMTP catcher, let me know if you already have it installed or want me to walk through getting it set up first.

Tool result
Gefunden Mailpit [axllent.mailpit]

Version: 1.29.5

Herausgeber: Ralph Slooten

Herausgeber-URL: https://github.com/axllent

Herausgeber-Support-URL: https://github.com/axllent/mailpit/issues

Autor: Ralph Slooten

Moniker: mailpit

Beschreibung:

  Mailpit is a small, fast, low memory, zero-dependency, multi-platform email testing tool & API for

  developers.

  

  It acts as an SMTP server, provides a modern web interface to view & test captured emails, and

  includes an API for automated integration testing.

  

  Mailpit was originally inspired by MailHog which is no longer maintained and hasn't seen active

  development or security updates for a few years now.

Startseite: https://github.com/axllent/mailpit

Lizenz: MIT

Lizenz-URL: https://github.com/axllent/mailpit/blob/develop/LICENSE

Copyright: Copyright (c) 2022-Now() Ralph Slooten

Copyright-URL: https://github.com/axllent/mailpit/blob/develop/LICENSE

Versionshinweise:

  ### Security

  

  - Add sandbox attribute to message iframe for extra layer of security (already protected via CSP headers)

  

  ### Feature

  

  - Add option to disable auto-VACUUMing of the SQLite database (#661)

  

  ### Chore

  

  - Update Go dependencies

  - Update node dependencies

URL der Versionshinweise: https://github.com/axllent/mailpit/releases/tag/v1.29.5

Markierungen:

  email-testing

  go

  golang

  mailpit

  pop3-server

  smtp-relay

  smtp-server

  smtp-testing

Installationsprogramm:

  Installertyp: portable (zip)

  Installer-URL: https://github.com/axllent/mailpit/releases/download/v1.29.5/mailpit-windows-amd64.zip

  Sha256-Installer: df62b2e446b7037c30d0cc3eccd248f3b0efbd8ffc2472df9abf697376eada99

  Freigabedatum: 2026-03-29

  Unterstützte Offlineverteilung: true

Let's do a quick end-to-end sanity check — send a test email via Python's stdlib smtplib and confirm it shows up in Mailpit.

Claude12.07.2026 15:17
Bash
{
  "command": "python3 -c \"\nimport smtplib\nfrom email.mime.text import MIMEText\n\nmsg = MIMEText('This is a Mailpit connectivity test from the Nudge setup.')\nmsg['Subject'] = 'Mailpit test'\nmsg['From'] = 'nudge@example.com'\nmsg['To'] = 'you@example.com'\n\nwith smtplib.SMTP('localhost', 1025) as server:\n    server.send_message(msg)\nprint('sent')\n\"\ncurl -s http://localhost:8025/api/v1/messages | python3 -c \"import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print('messages_count:', d['messages_count']); print('subject:', d['messages'][0]['Subject'])\"",
  "description": "Send a test email via smtplib and verify Mailpit captured it"
}
Tool result
sent

messages_count: 1

subject: Mailpit test
Claude12.07.2026 15:17

Full pipeline works end-to-end: stdlib smtplib → Mailpit → visible via its API. Stopping it now since I don't want to leave background processes running unannounced.

Claude12.07.2026 15:17
Bash
{
  "command": "powershell -NoProfile -Command \"Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter \\\"Name='mailpit.exe'\\\" | ForEach-Object { Stop-Process -Id \\$_.ProcessId -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }; 'stopped'\"",
  "description": "Stop Mailpit"
}
Tool result
stopped
Claude12.07.2026 15:17

Mailpit's installed and verified working. Quick reference for running it yourself later:

powershell
mailpit
(You'll need a fresh terminal since the PATH update needs a shell restart to take effect — or use the full path: C:\Users\Summer\AppData\Local\Microsoft\WinGet\Packages\axllent.mailpit_Microsoft.Winget.Source_8wekyb3d8bbwe\mailpit.exe)
  • SMTP catcher: localhost:1025
  • Web UI to view captured mail: http://localhost:8025

I sent a real test email through it via Python's smtplib (the exact library the worker will use) and confirmed it showed up — so the full pipeline the worker needs for REVIEW-FUNC-003 is proven end-to-end already.

Ready to move into Step 6 whenever you want — the worker sends a weekly email listing open focus tasks via smtplib, tested against Mailpit.

Claude12.07.2026 15:20
Write
{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "content": "import json\nimport smtplib\nimport time\nimport urllib.error\nimport urllib.request\nfrom datetime import datetime\nfrom email.mime.text import MIMEText\nfrom email.utils import make_msgid\n\nCORE_APP_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:3000\"\nPOLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 30\n\nSMTP_HOST = \"localhost\"\nSMTP_PORT = 1025\nFROM_ADDRESS = \"nudge@localhost\"\nTO_ADDRESS = \"user@localhost\"\nREVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 60  # stands in for \"weekly\" during local testing\n\n\ndef log(message: str) -> None:\n    print(f\"[{datetime.now().isoformat(timespec='seconds')}] {message}\", flush=True)\n\n\ndef fetch_open_tasks() -> list[dict] | None:\n    url = f\"{CORE_APP_URL}/api/tasks\"\n    try:\n        with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=5) as response:\n            tasks = json.loads(response.read())\n    except urllib.error.URLError as error:\n        log(f\"Could not reach core-app at {CORE_APP_URL}: {error}\")\n        return None\n\n    return [task for task in tasks if task[\"status\"] == \"active\"]\n\n\ndef format_date(value: str) -> str:\n    return datetime.fromisoformat(value).strftime(\"%b %d, %Y %I:%M %p\")\n\n\ndef next_checkpoint(task: dict) -> str:\n    now = datetime.now()\n    checkpoint_1 = datetime.fromisoformat(task[\"checkpoint_1\"])\n    checkpoint_2 = datetime.fromisoformat(task[\"checkpoint_2\"])\n    if now < checkpoint_1:\n        return task[\"checkpoint_1\"]\n    if now < checkpoint_2:\n        return task[\"checkpoint_2\"]\n    return task[\"buffer_deadline\"]\n\n\ndef build_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> MIMEText:\n    task_ids = \",\".join(str(task[\"id\"]) for task in tasks)\n    subject = f\"Nudge Weekly Review [ids: {task_ids}]\"\n\n    lines = [\"Here are your currently open tasks:\", \"\"]\n    for task in tasks:\n        lines.append(f\"#{task['id']} — {task['name']}\")\n        lines.append(f\"    Status: {task['status']}\")\n        lines.append(f\"    Next checkpoint: {format_date(next_checkpoint(task))}\")\n        lines.append(\"\")\n    lines.append(\"Reply with the IDs of your top 3 priorities for this week.\")\n\n    message = MIMEText(\"\\n\".join(lines))\n    message[\"Subject\"] = subject\n    message[\"From\"] = FROM_ADDRESS\n    message[\"To\"] = TO_ADDRESS\n    message[\"Message-ID\"] = make_msgid()\n    return message\n\n\ndef send_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> None:\n    if not tasks:\n        log(\"No open tasks — skipping weekly review email.\")\n        return\n\n    message = build_review_email(tasks)\n    try:\n        with smtplib.SMTP(SMTP_HOST, SMTP_PORT, timeout=5) as server:\n            server.send_message(message)\n    except OSError as error:\n        log(f\"Could not send weekly review email via {SMTP_HOST}:{SMTP_PORT}: {error}\")\n        return\n\n    log(f\"Sent weekly review email ({len(tasks)} task(s)), subject: {message['Subject']}\")\n\n\ndef run() -> None:\n    log(f\"notification-worker started, polling {CORE_APP_URL} every {POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS}s\")\n    last_review_sent = None\n\n    while True:\n        tasks = fetch_open_tasks()\n        if tasks is not None:\n            names = [task[\"name\"] for task in tasks]\n            log(f\"{len(tasks)} open task(s): {names}\")\n\n            now = time.monotonic()\n            if last_review_sent is None or now - last_review_sent >= REVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS:\n                send_review_email(tasks)\n                last_review_sent = now\n\n        time.sleep(POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS)\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    run()\n"
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:25

Found a real (if minor) encoding bug: the raw bytes show 0x97 (Windows-1252's em-dash), not proper UTF-8 — Python defaults to the system codepage for stdout when it's redirected to a file on Windows, not UTF-8. The actual emails are fine (email's MIME encoding is independent of this), but the worker's own console logs would show mojibake for any non-ASCII text. Fixing it properly.

Claude12.07.2026 15:25

  "offset": 1,
  "limit": 6
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:27

Now let's write the step documentation.

Claude12.07.2026 15:28
Write
{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md",
  "content": "# Step 6: E-Mail-Versand und Mailpit-Anbindung\n\n**Status:** Abgeschlossen\n**Bezug:** REVIEW-FUNC-003. Setzt Mailpit voraus (siehe `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`, Abschnitt 2 — in dieser Session lokal installiert, siehe unten).\n\n---\n\n## Mailpit-Installation\n\nLokal per `winget install axllent.mailpit` installiert (offizielles Paket des Mailpit-Maintainers, MIT-lizenziert, Quelle `github.com/axllent/mailpit`). Startet zwei Dienste:\n- SMTP-Fänger auf Port `1025` (kein Auth, keine Verschlüsselung — reines lokales Test-Setup)\n- Web-UI/API auf Port `8025` (`http://localhost:8025`)\n\nStart: `mailpit` (nach Neustart der Shell, damit die PATH-Änderung greift) oder direkt über den vollen Pfad unter `%LOCALAPPDATA%\\Microsoft\\WinGet\\Packages\\axllent.mailpit_...\\mailpit.exe`.\n\n## Was gebaut wurde\n\n`notification-worker/worker.py` erweitert um:\n\n```python\nSMTP_HOST = \"localhost\"\nSMTP_PORT = 1025\nFROM_ADDRESS = \"nudge@localhost\"\nTO_ADDRESS = \"user@localhost\"\nREVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 60  # steht für \"wöchentlich\" im lokalen Testbetrieb\n\n\ndef build_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> MIMEText:\n    task_ids = \",\".join(str(task[\"id\"]) for task in tasks)\n    subject = f\"Nudge Weekly Review [ids: {task_ids}]\"\n    ...\n    message[\"Message-ID\"] = make_msgid()\n    return message\n\n\ndef send_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> None:\n    if not tasks:\n        log(\"No open tasks — skipping weekly review email.\")\n        return\n    message = build_review_email(tasks)\n    try:\n        with smtplib.SMTP(SMTP_HOST, SMTP_PORT, timeout=5) as server:\n            server.send_message(message)\n    except OSError as error:\n        log(f\"Could not send weekly review email via {SMTP_HOST}:{SMTP_PORT}: {error}\")\n        return\n    log(f\"Sent weekly review email ({len(tasks)} task(s)), subject: {message['Subject']}\")\n```\n\n### Eindeutige Referenz (REVIEW-FUNC-003)\n\nDie Anforderung erlaubt explizit zwei Varianten (\"Message-ID **oder** Task-IDs im Betreff\") — beide wurden umgesetzt:\n- **Task-IDs im Betreff** (`Nudge Weekly Review [ids: 2,1]`) ist die Variante, auf der die spätere Antwort-Zuordnung in Schritt 7 aufbauen wird — ein einfacher String-Match auf den Betreff ist robuster gegenüber Mailpits API (die eher auf Volltext-/Metadatensuche ausgelegt ist als auf klassisches IMAP-Header-Threading) und einfacher in der Prüfung zu erklären als E-Mail-Threading-Header.\n- **`Message-ID`-Header** wird zusätzlich korrekt über `email.utils.make_msgid()` gesetzt (Python fügt diesen Header sonst nicht automatisch hinzu) — technisch korrekter E-Mail-Aufbau, auch wenn die eigentliche Zuordnung über die Betreffzeile läuft.\n\n### \"Alle offenen Tasks\" und Checkpoint-Datum\n\nWiederverwendung derselben Filterlogik wie in Schritt 5 (`status == \"active\"`, siehe dortige Dokumentation zur \"Fokus-Tasks kommen erst in Schritt 8\"-Entscheidung). Für \"Checkpoint-Datum\" wird der jeweils **nächste noch nicht erreichte** Termin gezeigt (`next_checkpoint()`: Checkpoint 1, sonst Checkpoint 2, sonst die Buffer-Deadline) — bewusst dupliziert statt aus `core-app` importiert, weil der Worker als komplett eigenständiger Prozess ausschließlich über HTTP mit der Kern-App spricht (kein gemeinsames Python-Modul zwischen den Prozessen — unterstreicht die Prozess-Unabhängigkeit auch auf Code-Ebene, siehe `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`).\n\n### Sende-Rhythmus getrennt vom Polling\n\nDer Worker pollt weiterhin alle 30s (Schritt 5), verschickt die Review-Mail aber nur, wenn seit dem letzten Versand mindestens `REVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS` vergangen sind (in-memory-Zeitstempel `last_review_sent`, verglichen über `time.monotonic()`). Der Wert steht für die im Original-Spec beschriebene \"wöchentliche\" Kadenz — für den lokalen Testbetrieb auf 60s statt einer Woche gesetzt, damit sich das Verhalten in angemessener Zeit beobachten lässt. Kein Missbrauch von `time.sleep()`-Wartezeiten für eine \"echte\" Woche in Tests.\n\n### Fehlerbehandlung\n\nEin nicht erreichbares Mailpit (z. B. SMTP-Server nicht gestartet) wird als `OSError` abgefangen und geloggt, ohne den Worker-Prozess zum Absturz zu bringen — dieselbe Resilienz-Idee wie bei der Kern-App-Erreichbarkeit in Schritt 5, hier auf eine dritte, externe Abhängigkeit (den Mail-Server) angewendet.\n\n### Gefundener und behobener Encoding-Bug\n\nBeim Testen fiel auf, dass Log-Zeilen mit Sonderzeichen (Halbgeviertstrich `—`) in der umgeleiteten Log-Datei als `�` erschienen. Ursache: Python nutzt für `stdout` unter Windows standardmäßig die System-Codepage (hier `cp1252`) statt UTF-8, sobald die Ausgabe nicht an ein Terminal, sondern in eine Datei umgeleitet wird — der `—`-Charakter landete dadurch als Einzelbyte `0x97` (cp1252) statt als korrekte 3-Byte-UTF-8-Sequenz (`E2 80 94`) in der Datei. Behoben mit `sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding=\"utf-8\")` direkt nach den Imports. **Wichtig:** Betraf ausschließlich die lokalen Konsolen-Logs des Workers, nicht die tatsächlich versendeten E-Mails — Pythons `email`-Modul kodiert den Nachrichtentext unabhängig von der Konsolen-Codepage korrekt (im Mailpit-Test vor der Korrektur bereits als korrektes UTF-8 verifiziert).\n\n## Verifikation\n\nVollständiger lokaler Testlauf mit allen drei Prozessen (core-app, notification-worker, Mailpit):\n\n1. Zwei Test-Tasks angelegt, Worker gestartet → erste Review-Mail sofort verschickt (`Nudge Weekly Review [ids: 2,1]`), in Mailpit per API abgerufen und inhaltlich geprüft: korrekte Task-Liste mit ID, Status, nächstem Checkpoint-Datum, korrekt gesetzter `Message-ID`-Header.\n2. Zweiter Poll-Zyklus (30s später, insgesamt 30s seit letztem Versand) → **kein** erneuter Versand (Intervall-Sperre funktioniert, Mailpit-Nachrichtenzahl bleibt bei 1).\n3. Dritter Poll-Zyklus (60s seit letztem Versand) → erneuter Versand, Mailpit-Nachrichtenzahl steigt auf 2.\n4. Mailpit-Prozess beendet, während core-app und Worker weiterlaufen → nächster Versand-Versuch loggt sauber `Could not send weekly review email via localhost:1025: [WinError 10061] ...`, Worker-Prozess läuft unverändert weiter.\n5. Beide Test-Tasks auf `done` gesetzt → nächster Zyklus loggt `0 open task(s)` und `No open tasks — skipping weekly review email.` statt eine leere Mail zu verschicken.\n6. Encoding-Fix angewendet, Prozesse neu gestartet, derselbe Skip-Fall erneut ausgelöst → Log-Datei enthält jetzt die korrekten UTF-8-Bytes für den Halbgeviertstrich (per `od -c` gegen die Rohbytes verifiziert, nicht nur visuell).\n\nAlle Prozesse (core-app, Worker, Mailpit) danach gestoppt, Testdatenbank gelöscht.\n\n## Nächster Schritt\n\nSchritt 7: Regelbasiertes Reply-Parsing (REVIEW-FUNC-004, 005) — der Worker fragt per IMAP/POP3 gezielt nach Antworten auf die referenzierte Mail und extrahiert die Top-3-Priorisierung aus der Antwort.\n"
}
Tool result
File created successfully at: D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\docs\09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
Claude12.07.2026 15:28

Now let's commit and push.

Claude12.07.2026 15:28

Send\ncadence is tracked separately from the poll loop (in-memory\ntimestamp) to represent a weekly rhythm without literally waiting a\nweek during local testing.\n\nVerified against a locally installed Mailpit instance: email\ncontent and headers, interval gating (no duplicate sends within the\nwindow), graceful handling when Mailpit is unreachable (logs and\nkeeps polling, no crash), and the empty-task-list case (skips\ninstead of sending a blank email).\n\nAlso fixes a real encoding bug found during testing: Python defaults\nstdout to the system codepage instead of UTF-8 when redirected to a\nfile on Windows, so non-ASCII log characters were getting mangled.\nOnly affected the worker's own console logs, not the emails\nthemselves (the email library encodes independently of console\ncodepage).\nEOF\n)\" 2>&1\ngit push 2>&1",
  "description": "Commit and push Step 6"
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:28

Step 6 is committed and pushed, and everything's cleaned up (no stray processes left running). And you're welcome — glad it's coming together.

Summary:


  • [notification-worker/worker.py](notification-worker/worker.py) now sends the weekly review email over smtplib, with task IDs in the subject as the reply-matching reference and a proper Message-ID header

  • [docs/09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md](docs/09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md)

I ran the full three-process stack (core-app, worker, Mailpit) together and verified every behavior that matters: email content and headers land correctly in Mailpit, the send interval is properly gated (no duplicate sends on every 30s poll), it survives Mailpit being down without crashing, and it skips sending entirely when there are no open tasks instead of firing off a blank email.

One real bug caught along the way: Windows made Python default the worker's console logs to the system codepage instead of UTF-8 when output was redirected to a file, mangling non-ASCII characters like the em-dash. Fixed with sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding="utf-8") — worth knowing this only affected the worker's own debug logs, not the actual emails, since Python's email library encodes independently of console settings (I verified the email content was correct even before the fix).

Ready for Step 7: reply-parsing (REVIEW-FUNC-004/005) whenever you want to continue — that's where the worker checks for a reply to that referenced email and extracts a rule-based Top-3 priority list from it.

Du12.07.2026 15:34

quick checkin:

def build_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> MIMEText:
    task_ids = ",".join(str(task["id"]) for task in tasks)
    subject = f"Nudge Weekly Review [ids: {task_ids}]"

lines = ["Here are your currently open tasks:", ""]
    for task in tasks:
        lines.append(f"#{task['id']} — {task['name']}")
        lines.append(f"    Status: {task['status']}")
        lines.append(f"    Next checkpoint: {format_date(next_checkpoint(task))}")
        lines.append("")
    lines.append("Reply with the IDs of your top 3 priorities for this week.")


message = MIMEText("\n".join(lines))
    message["Subject"] = subject
    message["From"] = FROM_ADDRESS
    message["To"] = TO_ADDRESS
    message["Message-ID"] = make_msgid()
    return message

I don't see a cap on how many tasks it suggests from? Let's say the user has 20+ tasks, this could get long.

Suggestion: Tasks with deadline in next 14 days, if more than 10 show only 10 most urgent but in blocks of five for readabiltiy.

Tool result
166	---
167	
168	## Module 4: Weekly Review Loop
169	
170	### Problem it solves
171	Without a regular aggregation point, commitments live in scattered emails, chat messages, calendar events, and memory. The weekly review makes the invisible visible and forces a prioritization decision.
172	
173	### How it works
174	
175	**Monday morning email** (or notification — whatever channel you actually check):
176	- Lists all open tasks with their checkpoint dates and status
177	- Highlights anything overdue or approaching a checkpoint
178	- Shows the week's calendar commitments
179	- Flags any tasks that have been sitting with no artifact for more than one cycle
180	
181	**You reply with your top 3 priorities for the week.** This is the executive function moment — not reading, but *choosing*. The system doesn't let you say "all of them."
182	
183	**Those 3 get time-blocked in your calendar** (manually or auto-scheduled if calendar integration is available). Each block includes the micro-instructions or current status so you don't have to reconstruct context when the block arrives.
184	
185	**Wednesday check-in email:**
186	- Shows the 3 committed tasks
187	- For each: "Show artifact" / "I'm blocked (→ triage)" / "Pushed to later this week"
188	- Escalation triggers only when no interaction **and** a checkpoint or deadline is approaching. No interaction on a task that's comfortably ahead of schedule doesn't generate noise.
189	
190	**Friday summary:**
191	- What got done (with artifacts as evidence)
192	- What rolled over (auto-added to next Monday's list)
193	- Time tracker summary for the week (ready to export to company timesheet)
194	- Estimate accuracy: predicted vs. actual hours per task (builds calibration data over time)
195	
196	---
197	
198	## Module 5: Panic Mode
199	
200	### Problem it solves
201	When a situation has already passed the point of recovery through normal productivity, the system should stop optimizing for output and start optimizing for damage contro
… [truncated, 262 more chars]
Claude12.07.2026 15:35

Good catch, but this bumps into something I should flag rather than just implement: REVIEW-FUNC-003's text literally says *"eine E-Mail mit allen offenen Tasks"* (Must), and the original module spec is explicit about why — *"Lists all open tasks... makes the invisible visible... You reply with your top 3 priorities... The system doesn't let you say 'all of them.'"* The design intent is that full visibility is the point, and the forced-choice-of-3 in the *reply* is what's supposed to prevent overload — not pre-filtering what's shown. Silently capping to 10 would deviate from both the requirement text and that stated design rationale.

That said, your underlying concern is real: a wall of 20+ undifferentiated tasks is genuinely hard to read, especially for a tool whose whole premise is reducing cognitive load. I think there's a version of this that improves readability without dropping information:

  • Keep listing all open tasks (satisfies the Must-requirement and "make invisible visible" intent)
  • Sort by urgency — already halfway there, since /api/tasks already returns tasks ordered by buffer deadline
  • Format in blocks of five with spacing/headers for scannability (a pure formatting change, no information lost)
Claude12.07.2026 15:35

        {
          "label": "Show all, format better (Recommended)",

Sort by urgency, chunk into blocks of 5 with spacing for readability. No task ever silently missing from the email."
        },

          "label": "Cap and filter as proposed",

47	    if now < checkpoint_1:
48	        return task["checkpoint_1"]
49	    if now < checkpoint_2:
50	        return task["checkpoint_2"]
51	    return task["buffer_deadline"]

Claude12.07.2026 15:38
Edit
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "old_string": "def build_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> MIMEText:\n    task_ids = \",\".join(str(task[\"id\"]) for task in tasks)\n    subject = f\"Nudge Weekly Review [ids: {task_ids}]\"\n\n    lines = [\"Here are your currently open tasks:\", \"\"]\n    for task in tasks:\n        lines.append(f\"#{task['id']} — {task['name']}\")\n        lines.append(f\"    Status: {task['status']}\")\n        lines.append(f\"    Next checkpoint: {format_date(next_checkpoint(task))}\")\n        lines.append(\"\")\n    lines.append(\"Reply with the IDs of your top 3 priorities for this week.\")",
  "new_string": "TASKS_PER_BLOCK = 5\n\n\ndef build_review_email(tasks: list[dict]) -> MIMEText:\n    task_ids = \",\".join(str(task[\"id\"]) for task in tasks)\n    subject = f\"Nudge Weekly Review [ids: {task_ids}]\"\n\n    # tasks arrive sorted by urgency (core-app orders /api/tasks by buffer\n    # deadline), so grouping them in fixed-size blocks as-is means each\n    # block is also roughly a tier of urgency, not an arbitrary chunk.\n    lines = [\"Here are your currently open tasks:\", \"\"]\n    for index, task in enumerate(tasks):\n        if index > 0 and index % TASKS_PER_BLOCK == 0:\n            lines.append(\"-\" * 30)\n            lines.append(\"\")\n        lines.append(f\"#{task['id']} — {task['name']}\")\n        lines.append(f\"    Status: {task['status']}\")\n        lines.append(f\"    Next checkpoint: {format_date(next_checkpoint(task))}\")\n        lines.append(\"\")\n    lines.append(\"Reply with the IDs of your top 3 priorities for this week.\")"
}

Tool result
70	Beim Testen fiel auf, dass Log-Zeilen mit Sonderzeichen (Halbgeviertstrich `—`) in der umgeleiteten Log-Datei als `�` erschienen. Ursache: Python nutzt für `stdout` unter Windows standardmäßig die System-Codepage (hier `cp1252`) statt UTF-8, sobald die Ausgabe nicht an ein Terminal, sondern in eine Datei umgeleitet wird — der `—`-Charakter landete dadurch als Einzelbyte `0x97` (cp1252) statt als korrekte 3-Byte-UTF-8-Sequenz (`E2 80 94`) in der Datei. Behoben mit `sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding="utf-8")` direkt nach den Imports. **Wichtig:** Betraf ausschließlich die lokalen Konsolen-Logs des Workers, nicht die tatsächlich versendeten E-Mails — Pythons `email`-Modul kodiert den Nachrichtentext unabhängig von der Konsolen-Codepage korrekt (im Mailpit-Test vor der Korrektur bereits als korrektes UTF-8 verifiziert).
71	
72	## Verifikation
73	
74	Vollständiger lokaler Testlauf mit allen drei Prozessen (core-app, notification-worker, Mailpit):
75	
76	1. Zwei Test-Tasks angelegt, Worker gestartet → erste Review-Mail sofort verschickt (`Nudge Weekly Review [ids: 2,1]`), in Mailpit per API abgerufen und inhaltlich geprüft: korrekte Task-Liste mit ID, Status, nächstem Checkpoint-Datum, korrekt gesetzter `Message-ID`-Header.
77	2. Zweiter Poll-Zyklus (30s später, insgesamt 30s seit letztem Versand) → **kein** erneuter Versand (Intervall-Sperre funktioniert, Mailpit-Nachrichtenzahl bleibt bei 1).
78	3. Dritter Poll-Zyklus (60s seit letztem Versand) → erneuter Versand, Mailpit-Nachrichtenzahl steigt auf 2.
79	4. Mailpit-Prozess beendet, während core-app und Worker weiterlaufen → nächster Versand-Versuch loggt sauber `Could not send weekly review email via localhost:1025: [WinError 10061] ...`, Worker-Prozess läuft unverändert weiter.
80	5. Beide Test-Tasks auf `done` gesetzt → nächster Zyklus loggt `0 open task(s)` und `No open tasks — skipping weekly review email.` statt eine leere Mail zu verschicken.
81	6. Encoding-Fix angewendet, Prozesse neu gestartet, derselbe Skip-Fa
… [truncated, 256 more chars]

Edit
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md",
  "old_string": "Alle Prozesse (core-app, Worker, Mailpit) danach gestoppt, Testdatenbank gelöscht.",
  "new_string": "Alle Prozesse (core-app, Worker, Mailpit) danach gestoppt, Testdatenbank gelöscht.\n\n## Nachtrag aus der Nutzerinnen-Review: Skalierung bei vielen offenen Tasks\n\n**Aufgeworfen:** `build_review_email()` hatte keine Obergrenze — bei 20+ offenen Tasks würde die Mail zu einer unübersichtlichen Textwand. Vorschlag der Nutzerin: nur Tasks mit Deadline in den nächsten 14 Tagen, bei mehr als 10 nur die 10 dringendsten, in Fünferblöcken.\n\n**Analyse:** REVIEW-FUNC-003 verlangt wörtlich \"eine E-Mail mit **allen** offenen Tasks\" (Must). Das Original-Spec (`adhd-proof-workflow-spec_v2.md`, Modul 4) begründet das explizit: *\"Lists all open tasks... makes the invisible visible... You reply with your top 3 priorities... The system doesn't let you say 'all of them.'\"* Der Überlastungsschutz ist im Design bewusst auf die **Antwort** verlagert (Zwang zur Wahl von genau 3), nicht auf die Anzeige — vollständige Sichtbarkeit ist hier der Punkt, nicht ein Nebeneffekt. Ein stiller Cutoff bei 10 Tasks würde sowohl vom Anforderungstext als auch von dieser Design-Begründung abweichen.\n\n**Entscheidung (mit Nutzerin abgestimmt):** Weiterhin **alle** offenen Tasks auflisten, aber lesbarer formatieren:\n- Tasks erscheinen bereits nach Dringlichkeit sortiert (`/api/tasks` ordnet nach Buffer-Deadline, diese Reihenfolge bleibt durch den Listen-Filter in `fetch_open_tasks()` erhalten).\n- Neue Konstante `TASKS_PER_BLOCK = 5`: nach jeweils 5 Tasks wird eine Trennlinie (`---...`) eingefügt — rein visuelle Gliederung, keine Task fällt weg. Getestet mit 12 Tasks: korrekte Blöcke 5/5/2, durchgehend nach Dringlichkeit sortiert.\n\n**Zurückgestellter Alternativvorschlag (für später, falls sich in der Praxis ein echtes Bedürfnis zeigt):** 14-Tage-Fenster + Cap bei 10 dringendsten Tasks, wie ursprünglich vorgeschlagen. Nutzerin vermutet, dass sich dieses Muster im echten Gebrauch von selbst ergeben könnte (z. B. durch manuelles Filtern/Ignorieren weit entfernter Tasks) und dass die geplante \"deadline-lose Tasks\"-Erweiterung (siehe `05-step2-task-model.md`, Abschnitt \"Scope-Frage während Review\") die Größe der aktiven Task-Liste ohnehin reduzieren dürfte, sobald Backlog-Einträge nicht mehr als reguläre Tasks mitgezählt werden. Explizit als möglicher späterer Anforderungs-Change festgehalten, kein aktueller Widerspruch zu REVIEW-FUNC-003."
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:40

Committed and pushed, everything cleaned up. Summary of the fix:

  • Kept listing all open tasks (REVIEW-FUNC-003 explicitly requires this, and the original spec's own rationale puts the overload-safeguard on the *reply* — forced choice of exactly 3 — not on what's displayed)
  • Added TASKS_PER_BLOCK = 5: a divider line after every 5 tasks, relying on the existing urgency sort from /api/tasks
  • Tested with 12 tasks — clean 5/5/2 split, still sorted soonest-first, nothing dropped
  • Documented your original 14-day-window + top-10 proposal as a deliberately deferred alternative in [docs/09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md](docs/09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md), noting your hunch that it'll surface as a real need once usage patterns show it and that the future deadline-less-tasks feature might reduce the active-list bulk anyway

Ready for Step 7: reply-parsing (REVIEW-FUNC-004/005) whenever you want to continue.

Du12.07.2026 15:41

can you give me a how-to so I can check the functionality myself?

Claude12.07.2026 15:42
Write
{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\how-to-run-and-test-locally.md",
  "content": "# How To: Run and Test Nudge Locally\n\nReference guide for manually exercising everything built so far (Schritte 1–6). Update this as new features land — it's meant to stay current, not a one-time step record like the numbered docs.\n\n---\n\n## 1. Start all three processes\n\nThree separate terminals, since Teil B (`core-app`) and Teil C (`notification-worker`) are independent processes by design, plus Mailpit as the local SMTP catcher.\n\n**Terminal 1 — core-app:**\n```powershell\ncd D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\core-app\n.venv\\Scripts\\python.exe app.py\n```\n→ `http://127.0.0.1:3000`\n\n**Terminal 2 — notification-worker:**\n```powershell\ncd D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\n.venv\\Scripts\\python.exe worker.py\n```\n→ polls core-app every 30s, sends the review email every 60s (test-only interval — see note below)\n\n**Terminal 3 — Mailpit:**\n```powershell\nmailpit\n```\n(if the `mailpit` command isn't found, your shell hasn't picked up the PATH change yet — use the full path: `C:\\Users\\Summer\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\WinGet\\Packages\\axllent.mailpit_Microsoft.Winget.Source_8wekyb3d8bbwe\\mailpit.exe`)\n→ SMTP catcher on port `1025`, web UI at `http://localhost:8025`\n\nYou don't strictly need all three running to test core-app's UI — only the worker and Mailpit steps below need them.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Core-app walkthrough (Teil B — Checkpoint system)\n\n- **Dashboard** — `http://127.0.0.1:3000/`\n  Shows the one active task with the nearest checkpoint, plus a short list of the rest. Empty state if you have no active tasks yet.\n\n- **All Tasks + Add Task** — `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tasks`\n  Create a task here (name + deadline). Check it appears in the list with a status badge and buffer deadline.\n\n- **Task Detail** — click any task\n  - Verify the four computed dates make sense: external deadline, buffer deadline (70% of the way there), checkpoint 1 (50%) and checkpoint 2 (75%) — both between creation and the buffer deadline, not the external deadline.\n  - Buffer progress bar should be near 0% for a freshly created task.\n  - \"Check In\" button: outline style normally, switches to filled \"Checkpoint reached — Check In\" once `now` passes checkpoint 1 or 2.\n\n- **Check-in screen** — click \"Check In\"\n  - **Submit Proof**: choose a file, submit. Confirm it shows up under Attachments on the task detail page and gets a line in the Activity log. Click the filename to confirm it opens/downloads. Try the delete (trash) icon too.\n  - **I'm Blocked** → goes to the Triage screen.\n\n- **Triage screen**\n  - **Start 10-Minute Timer**: redirects to a countdown page, ticks down live. Status should stay `active`, Activity log gets a \"Started a 10-minute focus timer\" entry.\n  - **Stuck / Waiting / Scope-deadline**: clicking one reveals a prefilled draft (recipient blank, subject/body templated from the task name). Fill in any recipient email, click \"Send & Pause Task\".\n    **Important:** this opens your **real default mail client** via a `mailto:` link — not Mailpit. That's intentional (`CHKP-FUNC-008`): blocker drafts are a person-to-person email Nudge never touches, completely separate from the worker's weekly review email in section 3 below. Don't be surprised if Outlook/Mail/etc. pops up.\n  - Confirm the task's status flips to `On Hold` and a \"Resume Task\" button appears on its detail page.\n\n- **Status actions** on the task detail page: \"Mark Complete\" → \"Reopen Task\", \"Resume Task\" for on-hold tasks. Each should log an Activity entry and update the status badge.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Notification-worker + Mailpit walkthrough (Teil C — Weekly review)\n\n1. With all three processes running and at least one active task, watch **Terminal 2** — you should see log lines like:\n   ```\n   [...] N open task(s): [...]\n   [...] Sent weekly review email (N task(s)), subject: Nudge Weekly Review [ids: ...]\n   ```\n2. Open `http://localhost:8025` (Mailpit's web UI) in a browser and open that email.\n   - Subject contains the task IDs (the \"unique reference\" for later reply-matching).\n   - Body lists every open task, sorted soonest-checkpoint-first, in blocks of 5 separated by a divider line if you have more than 5.\n3. **Process-independence check:** stop core-app (Ctrl+C in Terminal 1). Watch Terminal 2 — the worker should log a connection error on its next poll but keep running, not crash. Restart core-app; the worker reconnects on its own next cycle, no restart needed.\n4. Similarly, you can stop Mailpit and confirm the worker logs an SMTP error instead of crashing when it tries to send.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Shutting down\n\n`Ctrl+C` in each terminal. One quirk worth knowing: core-app runs with `debug=True`, and Flask's auto-reloader spawns a child process — `Ctrl+C` in the terminal should take both down together, but if you ever kill it via Task Manager instead, look for **two** `python.exe` entries under `core-app` (and, separately, the worker sometimes shows as two processes too — a Git-Bash/Windows quirk, harmless).\n\n---\n\n## Useful resets\n\n- **Fresh database:** delete `core-app\\nudge.db` (recreated automatically on next `app.py` start).\n- **Fresh uploads:** delete the `core-app\\uploads\\` folder.\n- **Faster/slower worker cadence:** `POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS` and `REVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS` at the top of `notification-worker\\worker.py`. The 60s review-email interval is a stand-in for \"weekly\" — fine for local testing, would need to become a real weekly duration for actual use.\n"
}

Claude12.07.2026 15:42

I wrote this up as [docs/how-to-run-and-test-locally.md](docs/how-to-run-and-test-locally.md) so you have it as a living reference, not just a one-off answer. Quick summary:

Start three terminals:

powershell
# Terminal 1
cd D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\core-app
.venv\Scripts\python.exe app.py


Terminal 2

cd D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\notification-worker
.venv\Scripts\python.exe worker.py

Terminal 3

mailpit

Then walk through:
1. core-app (http://127.0.0.1:3000) — create a task on All Tasks, open its detail page, check the computed dates, try Check-In → Submit Proof, and separately Check-In → I'm Blocked → Triage (timer option, then a message-draft option → Send & Pause Task)
2. Worker + Mailpit (http://localhost:8025) — watch Terminal 2's logs for the poll/send cycle, then check Mailpit's web UI for the actual weekly review email

One thing worth knowing before you test the triage flow: "Send & Pause Task" opens your real default mail client (a mailto: link), not Mailpit — that's intentional, blocker drafts are person-to-person and never touch Nudge's backend. Mailpit only catches the worker's weekly review email, which is a completely separate mechanism. Don't be surprised if Outlook or Mail pops up when you click it.

The doc also covers process-independence testing (kill core-app or Mailpit while the worker's running, watch it survive) and how to reset your test data. Want me to commit this to the repo, or keep it local for now since it's more of a personal reference than exam material?

  "content": "# Backlog: Bewusst zurückgestellte Ideen\n\nSammelstelle für Punkte, die während des Baus aufkamen, aber bewusst nicht in den MVP-Scope gezogen wurden — kein Vollständigkeits-Versäumnis, sondern dokumentierte Entscheidung (siehe `03-mvp-requirements-checklist.md`, Abschnitt \"Bewusst nicht atomarisiert\" für dasselbe Prinzip). Jeder Eintrag verlinkt zurück zu der Stelle, an der er aufkam, für den vollen Kontext. Lebendes Dokument — bei Bedarf ergänzen, bei Umsetzung hierher als \"erledigt\" markieren oder entfernen.\n\n---\n\n- [ ] **HTML-formatierte Review-Mail** statt reinem Plaintext (`notification-worker/worker.py`, `build_review_email`). Aufgeworfen nach Schritt 6. Würde z. B. echte visuelle Blöcke statt Trennlinien-Zeichen, anklickbare Task-Links zurück in die Kern-App etc. ermöglichen. Reiner Plaintext war für den MVP bewusst einfacher (kein HTML-Templating im Worker nötig, in jedem Mail-Client lesbar, leicht in der Prüfung zu erklären).\n\n- [ ] **14-Tage-Fenster + Cap bei 10 dringendsten Tasks** für die Review-Mail, falls sich in der Praxis zeigt, dass die reine Block-Formatierung (Schritt 6) bei sehr vielen offenen Tasks nicht ausreicht. Siehe `09-step6-email-and-mailpit.md`, Abschnitt \"Nachtrag aus der Nutzerinnen-Review\" für die volle Abwägung gegen REVIEW-FUNC-003.\n\n- [ ] **Deadline-lose Tasks** (Backlog-/Ideen-Objekttyp, nimmt nicht am Checkpoint-/Buffer-/Eisenhower-Mechanismus teil). Erste Designidee: automatischer Wiedervorlage-Reminder ca. einen Monat nach Anlage. Siehe `05-step2-task-model.md`, Abschnitt \"Scope-Frage während Review\".\n\n- [ ] **KI-gestütztes Reply-Parsing** (lokales Modell via Ollama) als dritter, unabhängiger Prozess, ergänzend zum regelbasierten Parsing aus Schritt 7 — nicht als Ersatz. Siehe `00-kickoff-briefing.md`, Abschnitt 7.\n\n- [ ] **Settings-Screen** (SMTP-Konfiguration fürs UI statt hartkodierter Konstanten in `worker.py`, Checkpoint-Intervall-Overrides, Urgent-Schwellenwert, Accountability-Buddy-Kontakt). Aktuell kein eigener Bau-Schritt dafür vorgesehen; SMTP-Zugangsdaten und Review-Intervall stehen bislang als Konstanten direkt im Worker-Code. Bezug: `02c-screen-audit-and-design-decisions.md` (welche Teile von `nudge_settings` grundsätzlich übernehmbar wären), ESC-FUNC-003/004 in `03-mvp-requirements-checklist.md`.\n\n- [ ] **Eigener `/health`-Endpunkt für den Worker** (Ausbaustufe 2 des Unabhängigkeits-Nachweises). Aktuell reicht die manuelle Log-Beobachtung (Stufe 1) für die Abgabe. Siehe `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`, Abschnitt 3, und `08-step5-notification-worker-skeleton.md`.\n\n- [ ] **Umbenennen von Attachments** im Task-Detail. View + Delete decken das ursprüngliche Problem (unbrauchbare Originaldateinamen) ausreichend ab; Rename wäre zusätzlicher Komfort, kein Blocker.\n\n- [ ] **Docker** als spätere Portfolio-Politur (nicht Voraussetzung für den Distributed-Nachweis). Siehe `00-tooling-and-process-independence.md`, Abschnitt 1.\n\n- [ ] **ESC-FUNC-006** (Could) — Pro-Task individualisierbare Urgent-Schwellenwerte, statt nur eines globalen Default-Werts.\n\n- [ ] **ESC-FUNC-007** (Could) — Live-Neuberechnung des Eisenhower-Quadranten bei jedem App-Öffnen statt nur im täglichen Cron-Durchlauf.\n"
}

Mailpit is an email testing tool for developers.

It acts as an SMTP server, and provides a web interface to view all captured emails.

Documentation:
  https://github.com/axllent/mailpit
  https://mailpit.axllent.org/docs/

Usage:
  mailpit [flags]
  mailpit [command]

Available Commands:
  dump        Dump all messages from a database to a directory
  ingest      Ingest a file or folder of emails for testing
  readyz      Run a healthcheck to test if Mailpit is running
  reindex     Reindex the database
  sendmail    A sendmail command replacement for Mailpit
  version     Display the current version & update information

Flags:
  -d, --database string                   Database to store persistent data
      --disable-wal                       Disable WAL for local database (allows NFS mounted DBs)
      --disable-version-check             Disable version update checking
      --disable-auto-vacuum               Disable auto-VACUUM for the database
      --compression int                   Compression level to store raw messages (0-3) (default 1)
      --label string                      Optional label identify this Mailpit instance
      --tenant-id string                  Database tenant ID to isolate data
  -m, --max int                           Max number of messages to store (default 500)
      --max-age string                    Max age of messages in either (h)ours or (d)ays (eg: 3d)
      --use-message-dates                 Use message dates as the received dates
      --ignore-duplicate-ids              Ignore duplicate messages (by Message-ID)
      --log-file string                   Log output to file instead of stdout
  -q, --quiet                             Quiet logging (errors only)
  -v, --verbose                           Verbose logging
  -l, --listen string                     HTTP bind interface & port for UI (default "[::]:8025")
      --webroot string                    Set the webroot for web UI & API (default "/")
      --ui-auth-file s

Claude12.07.2026 16:04

  "command": "\"/c/Users/Summer/AppData/Local/Microsoft/WinGet/Packages/axllent.mailpit_Microsoft.Winget.Source_8wekyb3d8bbwe/mailpit.exe\" --help 2>&1 | grep -i -E \"imap|pop3\"",

Tool result
      --pop3 string                       POP3 server bind interface and port (default "[::]:1110")
      --pop3-auth-file string             A password file for POP3 server authentication (enables POP3 server)
      --pop3-tls-cert string              Optional TLS certificate for POP3 server - requires pop3-tls-key
      --pop3-tls-key string               Optional TLS key for POP3 server - requires pop3-tls-cert
Claude12.07.2026 16:04

Mailpit doesn't run an IMAP server at all — only SMTP (send), POP3 (retrieve), and its own REST API. This directly collides with the requirement text: REVIEW-FUNC-004 says *"per IMAP"* and REVIEW-NFR-001 names smtplib, imaplib specifically. Your own 00-tooling-and-process-independence.md actually half-anticipated this — it calls Mailpit's retrieval side a *"simulierter IMAP-Antwortzyklus"* — but didn't pin down exactly how.

Three real options for the local test loop:

1. poplib against Mailpit's POP3 server (my recommendation) — genuinely stdlib, a real mail-retrieval protocol talking to a real server, not a mock. Satisfies the *spirit* of REVIEW-NFR-001 (stdlib-only, targeted retrieval, no generic inbox) even though the literal text says IMAP. Swapping to real imaplib against a real account later is a small, well-understood change (same shape: connect → search → fetch → parse).
2. imaplib against a real email account (e.g., a Gmail app password) — matches the requirement text exactly, but needs your real credentials wired in, isn't testable against Mailpit at all, and is a bigger, riskier step to set up right now.
3. Mailpit's REST API via urllib — works, but isn't a mail protocol at all and would actually violate REVIEW-NFR-001 more than option 1 does (not smtplib/imaplib/poplib, just HTTP).

Claude12.07.2026 16:04
AskUserQuestion
{
  "questions": [
    {
      "question": "How should the worker fetch replies for local testing, given Mailpit has no IMAP server?",
      "header": "Reply-fetch protocol",
      "options": [
        {
          "label": "poplib against Mailpit (Recommended)",
          "description": "stdlib POP3, real protocol against Mailpit's actual POP3 server. Deviates from REVIEW-FUNC-004's literal 'per IMAP' wording, which I'd document as a testing-tool constraint. A real deployment against Gmail/Outlook would swap in imaplib with minimal code change."
        },
        {
          "label": "imaplib against a real account",
          "description": "Matches the requirement text exactly. Needs a real mailbox + app password wired into worker.py now, and can't be exercised against Mailpit — every test run would hit a real inbox."
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Claude12.07.2026 16:08
Edit
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "old_string": "import json\nimport smtplib\nimport sys\nimport time\nimport urllib.error\nimport urllib.request\nfrom datetime import datetime\nfrom email.mime.text import MIMEText\nfrom email.utils import make_msgid\n\nsys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\nCORE_APP_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:3000\"\nPOLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 30\n\nSMTP_HOST = \"localhost\"\nSMTP_PORT = 1025\nFROM_ADDRESS = \"nudge@localhost\"\nTO_ADDRESS = \"user@localhost\"\nREVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 60  # stands in for \"weekly\" during local testing",
  "new_string": "import email\nimport email.policy\nimport json\nimport poplib\nimport re\nimport smtplib\nimport sys\nimport time\nimport urllib.error\nimport urllib.request\nfrom datetime import datetime\nfrom email.mime.text import MIMEText\nfrom email.utils import make_msgid\n\nsys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding=\"utf-8\")\n\nCORE_APP_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:3000\"\nPOLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 30\n\nSMTP_HOST = \"localhost\"\nSMTP_PORT = 1025\nFROM_ADDRESS = \"nudge@localhost\"\nTO_ADDRESS = \"user@localhost\"\nREVIEW_EMAIL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 60  # stands in for \"weekly\" during local testing\n\n# Mailpit has no IMAP server (confirmed via `mailpit --help`) — only SMTP,\n# POP3, and its own REST API. REVIEW-FUNC-004/REVIEW-NFR-001 name IMAP\n# specifically, but poplib is the closest available stdlib equivalent that\n# still talks a real mail-retrieval protocol to a real server rather than a\n# REST mock. See docs/how-to-run-and-test-locally.md for how to enable\n# Mailpit's POP3 server with matching credentials.\nPOP3_HOST = \"localhost\"\nPOP3_PORT = 1110\nPOP3_USERNAME = \"nudge\"\nPOP3_PASSWORD = \"nudge-local-test\"\n\nREVIEW_SUBJECT_PATTERN = re.compile(r\"Nudge Weekly Review \\[ids: ([\\d,]+)\\]\")\nTOP_PRIORITY_COUNT = 3\n\nprocessed_reply_uids: set[str] = set()"
}
Tool result
The file D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\notification-worker\worker.py has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
Claude12.07.2026 16:08
Edit
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "old_string": "    log(f\"Sent weekly review email ({len(tasks)} task(s)), subject: {message['Subject']}\")\n\n\ndef run() -> None:",
  "new_string": "    log(f\"Sent weekly review email ({len(tasks)} task(s)), subject: {message['Subject']}\")\n\n\ndef parse_top_priorities(body: str, valid_ids: set[int]) -> list[int]:\n    priorities: list[int] = []\n    for match in re.finditer(r\"#?\\b(\\d+)\\b\", body):\n        candidate = int(match.group(1))\n        if candidate in valid_ids and candidate not in priorities:\n            priorities.append(candidate)\n        if len(priorities) == TOP_PRIORITY_COUNT:\n            break\n    return priorities\n\n\ndef check_for_replies() -> None:\n    try:\n        conn = poplib.POP3(POP3_HOST, POP3_PORT, timeout=5)\n        conn.user(POP3_USERNAME)\n        conn.pass_(POP3_PASSWORD)\n    except (OSError, poplib.error_proto) as error:\n        log(f\"Could not check for replies via POP3 at {POP3_HOST}:{POP3_PORT}: {error}\")\n        return\n\n    try:\n        _, uidl_lines, _ = conn.uidl()\n        for line in uidl_lines:\n            msg_num_str, uid = line.decode().split(\" \", 1)\n            if uid in processed_reply_uids:\n                continue\n            msg_num = int(msg_num_str)\n\n            # REVIEW-FUNC-004: only ever act on replies to our own\n            # referenced thread — TOP fetches headers only (0 body lines)\n            # so we can check the subject before pulling the full message.\n            _, header_lines, _ = conn.top(msg_num, 0)\n            headers = email.message_from_bytes(\n                b\"\\r\\n\".join(header_lines), policy=email.policy.default\n            )\n            subject = headers.get(\"Subject\", \"\")\n\n            match = REVIEW_SUBJECT_PATTERN.search(subject)\n            if not match:\n                processed_reply_uids.add(uid)\n                continue\n\n            valid_ids = {int(x) for x in match.group(1).split(\",\")}\n\n            _, full_lines, _ = conn.retr(msg_num)\n            full_message = email.message_from_bytes(\n                b\"\\r\\n\".join(full_lines), policy=email.policy.default\n            )\n            body_part = full_message.get_body(preferencelist=(\"plain\",))\n            body_text = body_part.get_content() if body_part else \"\"\n\n            priorities = parse_top_priorities(body_text, valid_ids)\n            processed_reply_uids.add(uid)\n\n            if priorities:\n                log(f\"Reply to '{subject}' parsed top priorities: {priorities}\")\n            else:\n                log(f\"Reply to '{subject}' matched but no valid task IDs found in the body\")\n    finally:\n        conn.quit()\n\n\ndef run() -> None:"
}

183	    last_review_sent = None
184

193	                send_review_email(tasks)
194	                last_review_sent = now
195

Claude12.07.2026 16:10

Found a real bug — Mailpit is a single unified mailbox with no separation between "sent" and "inbox," so the worker's own outgoing email (whose body also mentions #3, #2, #1 in the task list) got picked up as if it were a reply to itself. I need to require an actual Re: prefix, which is how real replies are distinguished from the original message.

Claude12.07.2026 16:10

134	        conn.pass_(POP3_PASSWORD)

137	        return

Claude12.07.2026 16:10
Edit
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\notification-worker\\worker.py",
  "old_string": "            subject = headers.get(\"Subject\", \"\")\n\n            match = REVIEW_SUBJECT_PATTERN.search(subject)\n            if not match:\n                processed_reply_uids.add(uid)\n                continue",
  "new_string": "            subject = headers.get(\"Subject\", \"\")\n\n            # Mailpit catches everything the worker sends AND receives in\n            # one shared mailbox (no separate \"Sent\" folder like a real\n            # account), so the review email we just sent would otherwise\n            # match its own reference pattern. Requiring \"Re:\" is what\n            # actually distinguishes an incoming reply from our own\n            # outgoing message in this local setup.\n            is_reply = subject.strip().lower().startswith(\"re:\")\n            match = REVIEW_SUBJECT_PATTERN.search(subject)\n            if not is_reply or not match:\n                processed_reply_uids.add(uid)\n                continue"
}

{
  "command": "$procs = Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter \"Name='python.exe'\" | Where-Object { $_.CommandLine -like '*core-app*' -or $_.CommandLine -like '*notification-worker*' }\n$mailpitProcs = Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter \"Name='mailpit.exe'\"\nforeach ($p in $procs) { Stop-Process -Id $p.ProcessId -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }\nforeach ($p in $mailpitProcs) { Stop-Process -Id $p.ProcessId -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }\nStart-Sleep -Seconds 1\n\"stopped $($procs.Count) app/worker processes and $($mailpitProcs.Count) mailpit processes\"",

}

Claude12.07.2026 16:14

{
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\how-to-run-and-test-locally.md"

Tool result
1	# How To: Run and Test Nudge Locally
2	
3	Reference guide for manually exercising everything built so far (Schritte 1–6). Update this as new features land — it's meant to stay current, not a one-time step record like the numbered docs.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Start all three processes
8	
9	Three separate terminals, since Teil B (`core-app`) and Teil C (`notification-worker`) are independent processes by design, plus Mailpit as the local SMTP catcher.
10	
11	**Terminal 1 — core-app:**
12	```powershell
13	cd D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\core-app
14	.venv\Scripts\python.exe app.py
15	```
16	→ `http://127.0.0.1:3000`
17	
18	**Terminal 2 — notification-worker:**
19	```powershell
20	cd D:\xampp\htdocs\nudge\notification-worker
21	.venv\Scripts\python.exe worker.py
22	```
23	→ polls core-app every 30s, sends the review email every 60s (test-only interval — see note below)
24	
25	**Terminal 3 — Mailpit:**
26	```powershell
27	mailpit
28	```
29	(if the `mailpit` command isn't found, your shell hasn't picked up the PATH change yet — use the full path: `C:\Users\Summer\AppData\Local\Microsoft\WinGet\Packages\axllent.mailpit_Microsoft.Winget.Source_8wekyb3d8bbwe\mailpit.exe`)
30	→ SMTP catcher on port `1025`, web UI at `http://localhost:8025`
31	
32	You don't strictly need all three running to test core-app's UI — only the worker and Mailpit steps below need them.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## 2. Core-app walkthrough (Teil B — Checkpoint system)
37	
38	- **Dashboard** — `http://127.0.0.1:3000/`
39	  Shows the one active task with the nearest checkpoint, plus a short list of the rest. Empty state if you have no active tasks yet.
40	
41	- **All Tasks + Add Task** — `http://127.0.0.1:3000/tasks`
42	  Create a task here (name + deadline). Check it appears in the list with a status badge and buffer deadline.
43	
44	- **Task Detail** — click any task
45	  - Verify the four computed dates make sense: external deadline, buffer deadline (70% of the way there), checkpoint 1 (50%) and checkpoint 2 (75%) — b
… [truncated, 3644 more chars]

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\how-to-run-and-test-locally.md",
  "old_string": "**Terminal 3 — Mailpit:**\n```powershell\nmailpit\n```\n(if the `mailpit` command isn't found, your shell hasn't picked up the PATH change yet — use the full path: `C:\\Users\\Summer\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\WinGet\\Packages\\axllent.mailpit_Microsoft.Winget.Source_8wekyb3d8bbwe\\mailpit.exe`)\n→ SMTP catcher on port `1025`, web UI at `http://localhost:8025`",
  "new_string": "**Terminal 3 — Mailpit:**\nPOP3 must be explicitly enabled (off by default) for Step 7's reply-checking to work — it needs a plain `username:password` auth file matching `worker.py`'s `POP3_USERNAME`/`POP3_PASSWORD` constants:\n```powershell\n\"nudge:nudge-local-test\" | Out-File -Encoding ascii C:\\path\\to\\pop3-auth.txt\nmailpit --pop3-auth-file C:\\path\\to\\pop3-auth.txt\n```\n(if the `mailpit` command isn't found, your shell hasn't picked up the PATH change yet — use the full path: `C:\\Users\\Summer\\AppData\\Local\\Microsoft\\WinGet\\Packages\\axllent.mailpit_Microsoft.Winget.Source_8wekyb3d8bbwe\\mailpit.exe`)\n→ SMTP catcher on port `1025`, POP3 on port `1110`, web UI at `http://localhost:8025`. Without `--pop3-auth-file`, Mailpit only starts SMTP + the web UI — the worker's reply-checking will log a connection-refused error every cycle."
}

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "D:\\xampp\\htdocs\\nudge\\docs\\how-to-run-and-test-locally.md",
  "old_string": "3. **Process-independence check:** stop core-app (Ctrl+C in Terminal 1). Watch Terminal 2 — the worker should log a connection error on its next poll but keep running, not crash. Restart core-app; the worker reconnects on its own next cycle, no restart needed.\n4. Similarly, you can stop Mailpit and confirm the worker logs an SMTP error instead of crashing when it tries to send.",
  "new_string": "3. **Process-independence check:** stop core-app (Ctrl+C in Terminal 1). Watch Terminal 2 — the worker should log a connection error on its next poll but keep running, not crash. Restart core-app; the worker reconnects on its own next cycle, no restart needed.\n4. Similarly, you can stop Mailpit and confirm the worker logs an SMTP error instead of crashing when it tries to send.\n5. **Reply-parsing (Step 7):** since there's no real inbox to reply from, simulate one — send an email via Python matching the pattern below (adjust the IDs to whatever your last review email actually referenced, visible in its subject in Mailpit's UI):\n   ```powershell\n   python -c \"\n   import smtplib\n   from email.mime.text import MIMEText\n   msg = MIMEText('I think #2 and #1 are the priorities this week.')\n   msg['Subject'] = 'Re: Nudge Weekly Review [ids: 2,1,3]'\n   msg['From'] = 'user@localhost'\n   msg['To'] = 'nudge@localhost'\n   with smtplib.SMTP('localhost', 1025) as s:\n       s.send_message(msg)\n   \"\n   ```\n   Watch Terminal 2 for `Reply to '...' parsed top priorities: [2, 1]` on the next poll cycle. Try an unrelated subject too (no `Re:` prefix, or a totally different subject) and confirm it's silently ignored — no log line at all, since the worker never acts on anything outside its own referenced thread (`REVIEW-FUNC-004`)."
}
